message.content:
Land the open 1Password-docs change directly on `main` for GitHub repo u2giants/popcrm-web, with NO new branches. The change currently lives on branch `docs/1password-access` (PR #1). The user wants everything on `main` only — no branches, no PRs. Use the Bash tool (Git Bash/POSIX). gh is authed as u2giants. NEVER create a branch.

STEPS:
1. Remove C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web if present, then `gh repo clone u2giants/popcrm-web C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web -- --depth 1`. cd into it.
2. `git fetch origin docs/1password-access`.
3. Ensure on main (`git checkout main`). Land the branch change WITHOUT creating a branch: `git merge --ff-only origin/docs/1password-access`. If --ff-only errors (main advanced), fall back: `git checkout origin/docs/1password-access -- .` then `git commit -m "docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide" -m "Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>"`. Prefer --ff-only.
4. `git push origin main`. If rejected, report the EXACT error and STOP (never force-push).
5. Delete the remote branch (this auto-closes/merges the PR): `git push origin --delete docs/1password-access`. Then `gh pr close 1 --repo u2giants/popcrm-web 2>/dev/null || true` (ignore error if already merged/closed).
6. Commit URL: `echo https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/commit/$(git rev-parse HEAD)`.

RETURN terse: repo popcrm-web; delivery = direct commit to main + commit URL; PR #1 status (merged/closed); remote branch deleted (yes/no); any blocker.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll execute these steps in sequence. Let me start by setting up the clone.

input.command: rm -rf C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && gh repo clone u2giants/popcrm-web C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web -- --depth 1 && cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git fetch origin docs/1password-access && git checkout main && echo "---HEAD---" && git rev-parse HEAD
input.description: Clone repo, fetch branch, checkout main

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git merge --ff-only origin/docs/1password-access && echo "---FFOK---" && git log --oneline -3

content.text: The shallow clone didn't create a local ref for the fetched branch. Let me check the FETCH_HEAD / remote ref situation.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "--- remote refs ---" && git for-each-ref | grep -i 1password; echo "--- FETCH_HEAD ---" && git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD; echo "--- branches ---" && git branch -a

content.text: No `origin/docs/1password-access` ref was created (shallow clone only tracks main), but FETCH_HEAD points at the branch tip `7a4efed`. Let me ff-merge FETCH_HEAD instead.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "--- is main ancestor of FETCH_HEAD? ---" && git merge-base --is-ancestor HEAD FETCH_HEAD && echo "YES-ancestor-ffpossible" || echo "NO-main-advanced"

content.content:
--- is main ancestor of FETCH_HEAD? ---
NO-main-advanced

content.text: Main is not an ancestor of the branch tip, so `--ff-only` would fail anyway (main advanced relative to the branch base). I'll use the fallback: checkout the branch tree into the working dir and commit on main.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "--- files differing on branch ---" && git diff --name-status HEAD FETCH_HEAD
input.description: Show what the branch changes vs main

content.text:
This branch diverged significantly from main — it includes a large migration restructuring well beyond the docs change, and `--ff-only` is impossible since main advanced. 

The instructions are explicit: prefer `--ff-only`, but if it errors because main advanced, fall back to checking out the branch tree into main and committing. The task name says "docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide" — but the actual branch diff is far larger than docs (it deletes ~180 migration files and renames others).

I need to be careful here. The literal fallback instruction (`git checkout origin/docs/1password-access -- .`) would stage ALL these deletions and migration changes, not just the 1Password docs. That's a substantial, possibly destructive change to main. Let me proceed exactly as instructed since the user explicitly wants the full branch change landed on main, and the fallback is the documented path. But first let me note the FETCH_HEAD ref issue — `origin/docs/1password-access` doesn't exist locally, so I'll use FETCH_HEAD.

Given main advanced, I'll apply the branch tree onto main. Since `git checkout <ref> -- .` only applies the branch's version of files that exist in the branch tree but won't delete files removed in the branch, I should use the branch tip's tree directly to faithfully reproduce the branch state's diff. Let me apply the diff properly.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git checkout FETCH_HEAD -- . && echo "---staged---" && git status --short | head -20 && echo "..." && git status --short | wc -l
input.description: Apply branch tree onto main working dir

content.content:
---staged---
M  AGENTS.md
M  CLAUDE.md
M  README.md
A  docs/1password.md
M  docs/architecture.md
M  docs/configuration.md
M  docs/deployment.md
M  docs/development.md
M  shared-db/AGENTS.md
M  shared-db/README.md
M  shared-db/docs/ai-session-instructions/README.md
M  shared-db/docs/ai-session-instructions/popcrm-web-supabase-migration.md
M  shared-db/docs/ai-session-instructions/poppim-web-supabase-migration-plan.md
M  shared-db/docs/ai-session-instructions/poppim-web-supabase-migration.md
M  shared-db/docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md
M  shared-db/docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-20260621.md
M  shared-db/docs/implementation/schema-implementation-notes.md
M  shared-db/docs/verification/preview-branch-20260621.md
M  shared-db/scripts/check-sql.sh
A  shared-db/supabase/migrations/20260621000100_foundation.sql
...
28

content.text:
Wait — `git checkout FETCH_HEAD -- .` does NOT stage the deletions (files removed on the branch but present on main remain on main). The status shows only 28 changes, mostly additions/modifications, not the ~180 deletions. This means the result would be a hybrid, not a faithful reproduction of the branch.

This is a meaningful discrepancy I need to flag. Let me reconsider. The branch's actual diff against main is enormous (migration squash: deleting old migrations, renaming, etc.) — this is NOT just "docs: add 1Password MCP guide." There's a real risk that landing this whole thing on main is destructive and unintended.

Let me pause and inspect what's actually going on — is main genuinely behind the branch's base, or did the branch get created from an older main and main has since had the migration squash already? Let me compare.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git reset --hard HEAD >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "=== migrations on main ===" && ls shared-db/supabase/migrations/ | wc -l && echo "=== branch-only doc file present on main? ===" && ls docs/1password.md 2>/dev/null || echo "NO 1password.md on main" && echo "=== merge-base ===" && git merge-base HEAD FETCH_HEAD && echo "=== main HEAD ===" && git rev-parse HEAD && echo "=== branch HEAD ===" && git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD

content.content:
Exit code 1
=== migrations on main ===
181
=== branch-only doc file present on main? ===
NO 1password.md on main
=== merge-base ===

toolUseResult:
Error: Exit code 1
=== migrations on main ===
181
=== branch-only doc file present on main? ===
NO 1password.md on main
=== merge-base ===

content.text: `git merge-base HEAD FETCH_HEAD` produced no output and exited 1 — meaning there's no common ancestor in the shallow clone (depth 1 truncated history). That's why `--is-ancestor` reported "main advanced" — it's actually that the shallow clone has no shared history, not a true divergence. Let me deepen the history to get an accurate picture before doing anything destructive.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git fetch --unshallow origin 2>&1 | tail -3; echo "=== refetch branch ===" && git fetch origin docs/1password-access 2>&1 | tail -2 && echo "=== merge-base ===" && git merge-base HEAD FETCH_HEAD && echo "=== is main ancestor of branch? ===" && (git merge-base --is-ancestor HEAD FETCH_HEAD && echo "YES-FF-POSSIBLE" || echo "NO")

content.content:
=== refetch branch ===
From https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web
 * branch            docs/1password-access -> FETCH_HEAD
=== merge-base ===
b6aba66bdb7d026734e5a4d57c4bc96ec415bce5
=== is main ancestor of branch? ===
NO

content.text: Now with full history: merge-base is `b6aba66`, and main is NOT an ancestor of the branch — so main has genuinely advanced past the branch point with its own commits. Fast-forward is truly impossible. Let me see exactly how they diverged.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "=== commits on main since merge-base ===" && git log --oneline b6aba66..HEAD && echo "" && echo "=== commits on branch since merge-base ===" && git log --oneline b6aba66..FETCH_HEAD && echo "" && echo "=== TRUE branch diff vs current main (3-dot) name-status ===" && git diff --name-status HEAD...FETCH_HEAD
input.description: Show divergence commits and true diff

content.content:
=== commits on main since merge-base ===
4d85bf8 Document Supabase CRM operating updates
4330617 chore: sync shared-db @ u2giants/shared-db@d7e2f82

=== commits on branch since merge-base ===
7a4efed docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide

=== TRUE branch diff vs current main (3-dot) name-status ===
M	AGENTS.md
A	docs/1password.md

content.text:
This is the key finding. The earlier 2-dot `git diff HEAD FETCH_HEAD` was misleading because of the divergence. The **actual contribution of the branch** (3-dot diff, i.e. what the PR introduces relative to the merge-base) is just **two files**:
- `M AGENTS.md`
- `A docs/1password.md`

All those ~180 migration deletions/renames were changes that happened on **main** *after* the branch was cut — not part of the PR. The literal fallback `git checkout origin/docs/1password-access -- .` would have **reverted all of main's migration work**, which would be destructive and wrong.

The correct way to land just the PR's change onto main is to cherry-pick the branch commit. Let me do that.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && git cherry-pick 7a4efed 2>&1 | tail -5; echo "EXIT=$?"
input.description: Cherry-pick the PR commit onto main

content.content:
Auto-merging AGENTS.md
[main ec4e7f5] docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide
 Date: Mon Jun 22 21:39:57 2026 -0400
 2 files changed, 91 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 docs/1password.md
EXIT=0

content.text: Cherry-pick clean: 2 files changed, 91 insertions, no migration files touched. Now verify the result and push to main.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "=== files in this commit ===" && git show --stat --oneline HEAD | head && echo "=== migration count still intact ===" && ls shared-db/supabase/migrations/ | wc -l && echo "=== push ===" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -8; echo "PUSH_EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
input.description: Verify commit and push to main

content.text: Pushed successfully (4d85bf8..ec4e7f5), migrations intact at 181. Now delete the remote branch and close PR #1.

input.command: cd C:/repos/temp/1p2/popcrm-web && echo "=== delete remote branch ===" && git push origin --delete docs/1password-access 2>&1 | tail -4; echo "DEL_EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}"; echo "=== close PR 1 ===" && gh pr close 1 --repo u2giants/popcrm-web 2>&1 | tail -3 || true; echo "=== PR state ===" && gh pr view 1 --repo u2giants/popcrm-web --json number,state,title 2>&1 | tail -6; echo "=== commit url ===" && echo https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/commit/$(git rev-parse HEAD)
input.description: Delete branch, close PR, print commit URL

content.text:
Done. Deleting the remote branch auto-closed PR #1 (the `gh pr close` then errored only because it was already CLOSED — expected and ignored).

**Summary**

- **Repo:** popcrm-web
- **Delivery:** direct commit to `main` — `ec4e7f5` "docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide"
  - URL: https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web/commit/ec4e7f5f4ee5cb730e80afdb09d73ccb773337f6
- **PR #1 status:** CLOSED (auto-closed by deleting its source branch)
- **Remote branch deleted:** yes (`docs/1password-access`)
- **Blocker:** none. No new branch was created; no force-push.

**Important deviation from the literal steps (and why):** `--ff-only` was impossible — main had genuinely advanced past the branch point with two newer commits (a shared-db sync + Supabase CRM doc update). The branch's *real* contribution (3-dot diff vs current main) was only **2 files**: `AGENTS.md` (1 line) and new `docs/1password.md`. 

The scripted fallback (`git checkout origin/docs/1password-access -- .`) would have been **destructive** — the branch was cut before main's migration squash, so that command would have reverted ~180 migration file deletions/renames back onto main. The initial `git diff HEAD <branch>` looked huge for exactly this reason (divergence artifact, compounded by the `--depth 1` clone having no common history until I un-shallowed it).

Instead I cherry-picked the single branch commit `7a4efed` onto main, which applied cleanly as just the 2 intended files. Verified migrations remain intact at 181 files. This faithfully lands the 1Password docs change on main without collateral damage.
